how do neurons form new connections in brain plasticity? i've been reading about brain plasticity and how the brain can "rewire" itself. one of the things that is not clear to me - how neurons can establish new connections. does this rewiring mean that neurons can "disconnect" from other neurons to reuse existing dendrites? or do neurons grow new dendrites to make these new connections, adding to existing ones? thank you for your input! how do neurons form new connections in brain plasticity? i've been reading about brain plasticity and how the brain can "rewire" itself. one of the things that is not clear to me - how neurons can establish new connections. does this rewiring mean that neurons can "disconnect" from other neurons to reuse existing dendrites? or do neurons grow new dendrites to make these new connections, adding to existing ones? thank you for your input! neurons form new connections during brain plasticity through several remarkable processes. brain plasticity, or neuroplasticity, refers to the brain's ability to change and adapt as a result of experience, learning, or injury. when the brain "rewires" itself, neurons establish new connections primarily in two ways:

first, neurons can strengthen their existing connections by increasing the efficiency or number of synapses—the tiny junctions where communication occurs between neurons. this often involves changes in synaptic strength, a process known as synaptic plasticity. for example, repeated stimulation of a specific neural pathway can result in more neurotransmitters being released and a changes occur in brain wiring, modifying the strength of connections between neurons. this form of neuroplasticity can involve adding or removing new synapses. ...the formation of new connections between specific subsets of neurons.

by what mechanism can hypoglycemia induce fainting? hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) is said to be among the potential causes of human-faintings. i was wondering by which mechanism this happens. google search results are abundant; there are lists of various causes and solutions, but i couldn't find a direct answer to my question. is it a controlled reduction of heart function to save energy? is it an uncontrolled shock reaction (malfunction)? i'm looking for an answer along those lines. thanks. by what mechanism can hypoglycemia induce fainting? hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) is said to be among the potential causes of human-faintings. i was wondering by which mechanism this happens. google search results are abundant; there are lists of various causes and solutions, but i couldn't find a direct answer to my question. is it a controlled reduction of heart function to save energy? is it an uncontrolled shock reaction (malfunction)? i'm looking for an answer along those lines. thanks. hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) can induce fainting primarily through its effects on the brain. glucose is the main source of energy for brain cells, and when blood glucose levels drop significantly, the brain cannot function properly. the brain is highly sensitive to energy shortages because it has minimal energy reserves and cannot efficiently use alternative fuels in the short term. when hypoglycemia occurs, symptoms such as confusion, dizziness, and weakness may first appear. if blood sugar continues to fall, the brain's impaired function can result in loss of consciousness (syncope, or fainting). this is not usually a controlled, energy-saving process but rather reflex syncope occurs in response to a trigger due to dysfunction of the heart rate and blood pressure regulating mechanism. when heart rate slows or blood pressure drops, the resulting lack of blood to the brain causes fainting.

why do cyanide and arsenic smell like almonds and garlic, respectively? why do humans smell arsenic as garlic or cyanide as almonds? do both smells activate the same receptor? is there any reason why our brain interprets these smells the same? why do cyanide and arsenic smell like almonds and garlic, respectively? why do humans smell arsenic as garlic or cyanide as almonds? do both smells activate the same receptor? is there any reason why our brain interprets these smells the same? cyanide and arsenic are both toxic substances, and people often describe their odors as resembling almonds (for cyanide) and garlic (for arsenic). this occurs because specific volatile compounds are released from these substances: hydrogen cyanide (hcn) for cyanide, and various arsine or organoarsenic compounds for arsenic. 

hydrogen cyanide has a characteristic bitter almond smell that only a certain proportion of the human population can detect, due to genetic differences in odor receptors—specifically, a receptor called or2j3 is known to be involved in cyanide detection. for arsenic compounds, such as arsen hydrogen cyanide has a faint, bitter, almond odor that only about half of all people can smell. arsine has a very faint garlic odor detectable only at greater than fatal concentrations. in gas form, cyanide may give off a bitter almond smell, but not always.

if tumors have lots of mutations in them how is it the immune system can't detect them? if a cancerous tumor has a lot of mutations in them why can't the immune system detect them? if a person has cancer could this somehow alter the person's immune system so it doesn't function effectively to detect greatly mutated cells? does the fact that a tumor is 'made' of 'mutated' non-foreign human cells help it to be undetectable? if tumors have lots of mutations in them how is it the immune system can't detect them? if a cancerous tumor has a lot of mutations in them why can't the immune system detect them? if a person has cancer could this somehow alter the person's immune system so it doesn't function effectively to detect greatly mutated cells? does the fact that a tumor is 'made' of 'mutated' non-foreign human cells help it to be undetectable? although tumors often contain many mutations, they can still evade detection by the immune system for several reasons. first, even though cancer cells have mutations, these cells originate from the body’s own tissues. because of this, their markers—called antigens—often appear similar to normal “self” proteins, making it hard for the immune system to distinguish them as threats. in many cases, the mutations may produce altered or new antigens (neoantigens), but not all of these changes are noticeable or recognizable by immune cells. additionally, tumors can actively suppress or alter the immune response. some cancers produce signals that attract regulatory immune cells or se as part of its normal function, the immune system detects and destroys abnormal cells and most likely prevents or curbs the growth of many cancers.

how fast do we fall asleep? when looking at the process of sleeping, most research i find point to a group of mutually inhibitory cells which form a sort of biological "flip flop." there's cells that promote awakenness and cells that promote sleeping. the process we typically think of as "falling asleep" is getting to the point where this transition can occur, but i'm interested in the transition itself. how fast is this transition? in engineering, we'll typically measure the speed of such a transition as a 10-90 measurement: the time it takes to go from a 10% signal to a 90% signal. is there a similar measurement made in such biological systems? how fast do we fall asleep? when looking at the process of sleeping, most research i find point to a group of mutually inhibitory cells which form a sort of biological "flip flop." there's cells that promote awakenness and cells that promote sleeping. the process we typically think of as "falling asleep" is getting to the point where this transition can occur, but i'm interested in the transition itself. how fast is this transition? in engineering, we'll typically measure the speed of such a transition as a 10-90 measurement: the time it takes to go from a 10% signal to a 90% signal. is there a similar measurement made in such biological systems? the transition from wakefulness to sleep is a fascinating and complex process, and neuroscientists have studied its speed with increasing precision in recent years. as you mentioned, the brain contains populations of cells—those that promote arousal and those that promote sleep—that interact in a mutually inhibitory, “flip-flop” fashion. this arrangement allows for rapid and stable transitions between wake and sleep states, minimizing time spent in intermediate or unstable states.

to specifically address your question about the speed of this transition and whether it is quantified in a way similar to the engineering “10-90” transition measurement: yes, neuroscientific research has used analogous methods flip-flop

are there any documented cases of extinction of a species of fungus? are there any documented cases of extinction of a species of fungus? i imagine it would be hard to detect something like this. are there any documented cases of extinction of a species of fungus? are there any documented cases of extinction of a species of fungus? i imagine it would be hard to detect something like this. yes, there are documented cases of the extinction of fungal species, although such events can be difficult to confirm. fungi often have elusive, microscopic life stages and may remain undetected for long periods, making it challenging to declare a species truly extinct. however, some species have been formally assessed and listed as extinct by conservation organizations. a notable example is the fungus *rhizopogon oregonensis*, a truffle-like species from the pacific northwest of the united states, which was listed as extinct by the international union for conservation of nature (iucn). factors such as habitat loss, pollution, the introduction of invasive species, and are there any documented cases of extinction of a species of fungus? the observed hyperdiversity of the fungal community is in stark contrast to the low plant diversity found in interior alaskan picea mariana forests.

can a neuron make a synapse onto itself? i was wondering if a neuron can make a synapse to itself? i suspect that it would be extremely unusual for a neuron to do this. anyway, has anyone seen even a single instance of this? is the process by which a neuron 'knows' not to make a synapse onto itself is known? can a neuron make a synapse onto itself? i was wondering if a neuron can make a synapse to itself? i suspect that it would be extremely unusual for a neuron to do this. anyway, has anyone seen even a single instance of this? is the process by which a neuron 'knows' not to make a synapse onto itself is known? yes, a neuron can make a synapse onto itself, and this phenomenon is known as an "autapse." although it is relatively rare compared to connections between different neurons, autapses have been observed in various species and brain regions, particularly in certain types of inhibitory interneurons. an autapse is formed when a neuron's axon forms a synaptic connection with its own dendrite or soma. the exact functional role of autapses is still an area of ongoing research, but they are thought to play a part in modulating the firing properties of the neuron or in fine-tuning neural circuits.

as for whether neurons have mechanisms to specifically an autapse is a chemical or electrical synapse from a neuron onto itself. it can also be described as a synapse formed by the axon of a neuron on its own dendrites, in vivo or in vitro. each neuron has anywhere between a few to hundreds of thousands of synaptic connections, and these connections can be with itself, neighboring neurons, or neurons in other regions of the brain.

are there grass or fiber eating birds? my understanding, that may be wrong, is that cellulose/fibre has little nutritional value to many animals because it's hard to break down thus making consumption inefficient. however, ruminating mammals, other mammals such as black bears, and many insects such as grasshoppers digest grasses. are there any birds with the gut flora to break down fibre? or even some that eat grass but digest it in another way? are there grass or fiber eating birds? my understanding, that may be wrong, is that cellulose/fibre has little nutritional value to many animals because it's hard to break down thus making consumption inefficient. however, ruminating mammals, other mammals such as black bears, and many insects such as grasshoppers digest grasses. are there any birds with the gut flora to break down fibre? or even some that eat grass but digest it in another way? yes, there are birds that eat grass and fibrous plant material, although digesting cellulose is generally challenging for most birds. unlike ruminating mammals—such as cows and sheep—or certain insects, birds typically do not have gut flora capable of efficiently breaking down cellulose. their digestive systems are usually not adapted for extracting significant nutritional value directly from fiber. however, some birds have developed ways to incorporate grasses and fibrous plants into their diets.

among birds, a few notable groups consume grasses and fibrous material:

**1. waterfowl and geese:** many geese and some ducks are well-known grazers. they regularly eat grasses crude fiber

the primary way the eye distinguishes whether to focus nearer or further is through a combination of optical and neurological cues, mainly because the nature of the blur itself contains important information.

why can't our eyes smoothly transition from side to side without focusing on a moving object? why are we not able to slowly and smoothly look from side to side, or up and down in a single and smooth transition, given that we are able to do this if our eyes are focused on a moving object? why can't our eyes smoothly transition from side to side without focusing on a moving object? why are we not able to slowly and smoothly look from side to side, or up and down in a single and smooth transition, given that we are able to do this if our eyes are focused on a moving object? when we try to move our eyes slowly and smoothly from side to side or up and down without focusing on a moving object, we find it difficult to do so. this is because of the way our eye movement control systems are organized in the brain. our eyes are capable of two main types of voluntary movement: saccades and smooth pursuit. saccades are quick, jerky movements that shift our focus from one point to another, allowing us to scan our surroundings efficiently. on the other hand, smooth pursuit movements enable our eyes to smoothly follow a moving object.

smooth pursuit requires a moving target for the brain to track; the visual (spems) are observed when an individual tracks a slow-moving visual target during lucid rapid eye movement (rem) sleep dreaming. humans are typically unable to smoothly track slowly an imagined object in a sustained fashion, exhibiting instead saccadic eye movements.

if the brain has no pain receptors, how come you can get a headache? i've read many years ago in books, that the brain has no nerves on it, and if someone was touching your brain, you couldn't feel a thing. just two days before now, i had a very bad migraine, due to a cold. it's become better now, but when i had it i felt my head was going to literally split in half, as the pain was literally coming from my brain. so it lead me to the question: how come people can get headaches if the brain has no nerves? if the brain has no pain receptors, how come you can get a headache? i've read many years ago in books, that the brain has no nerves on it, and if someone was touching your brain, you couldn't feel a thing. just two days before now, i had a very bad migraine, due to a cold. it's become better now, but when i had it i felt my head was going to literally split in half, as the pain was literally coming from my brain. so it lead me to the question: how come people can get headaches if the brain has no nerves? it is true that the brain itself does not have pain receptors, which are the nerve endings responsible for sensing pain. this means that the actual brain tissue cannot feel pain, and this is why during certain types of brain surgery, patients sometimes remain awake, as the brain tissue can be operated on without the person feeling direct pain.

however, headaches, including migraines, are not caused by pain from the brain tissue itself. instead, headaches arise from pain in other structures in and around the head that do have pain receptors. these include the blood vessels, the membranes covering the brain and spinal cord (called the meninges), muscles of the scalp and ** - "the brain itself is not sensitive to pain, because it lacks pain receptors. however, several areas of the head and neck do have pain receptors and can thus sense pain. these include the extracranial arteries, middle meningeal artery, large veins, venous sinuses, cranial and spinal nerves, head and neck muscles, the meninges, falx cerebri, parts of the brainstem, eyes, ears, teeth, and lining of the

do teeth have the capacity to heal? could a cavity in a human tooth "heal over" or possibly fill back in? do teeth have the capacity to heal? could a cavity in a human tooth "heal over" or possibly fill back in? teeth have only a limited capacity to heal. the hard outer layer of a tooth, called enamel, does not contain living cells, so it cannot repair itself if it is damaged. however, in the very early stages of tooth decay, when the damage is limited to the demineralization of the enamel, the process can sometimes be reversed. this happens when minerals such as calcium and phosphate, along with fluoride from toothpaste or water, are redeposited into the enamel, helping to repair it. this is known as remineralization.

once a cavity forms—meaning the enamel has been worn away and a hole has developed—this do teeth have the capacity to heal? could a cavity in a human tooth 'heal over' or possibly fill back in? *fluoride treatments.* if your cavity just started, a fluoride treatment may help restore your tooth's enamel and can sometimes reverse a cavity in the very early stages. professional fluoride treatments contain more fluoride than the amount found in tap water, toothpaste and mouth rinses.

why does a coconut have exactly three "holes"? why do coconuts have exactly three "holes" why does a coconut have exactly three "holes"? why do coconuts have exactly three "holes" a coconut has exactly three “holes,” which are also called “eyes,” because of the way it develops on the palm tree. these three marks are actually the positions of the coconut’s germination pores. inside the coconut, there are three carpels, or ovules, that develop into three “eyes” after the flower is pollinated. usually, only one of these eyes is functional and allows the sprout to emerge when the seed germinates. the other two are non-functional. the three holes correspond to the places where the seed is weakest, allowing the growing plant to break through. this unique structure is a natural part of the coconut...will use its claws to rip off strips, always starting from the side with the three germination pores, the group of three small circles found on the outside of the coconut.

what creates the feeling of 'excess' blood pressure to an area of the body? if i hang upside down, and feel blood rushing to my head, what structures are actually responsible for me "feeling" this excess flow of blood? baroreceptors? mechanoreceptors? something else? regardless, how does this process actually work? how do i "feel" excess blood pressure to a certain area of my body? what creates the feeling of 'excess' blood pressure to an area of the body? if i hang upside down, and feel blood rushing to my head, what structures are actually responsible for me "feeling" this excess flow of blood? baroreceptors? mechanoreceptors? something else? regardless, how does this process actually work? how do i "feel" excess blood pressure to a certain area of my body? when you hang upside down and "feel" blood rushing to your head, this sensation is caused by an increase in blood pressure within the blood vessels of your head and face due to gravity. however, the feeling of "excess" blood pressure is not a direct sense—rather, it is a result of your body's complex sensory system detecting the changes in your blood vessels and tissues.

the structures involved in detecting changes in blood pressure include baroreceptors and mechanoreceptors. baroreceptors are specialized nerve endings located in the walls of certain blood vessels (primarily in the carotid sinuses of the neck and the aortic arch). baroreceptors are integral to the body's function: pressure changes in the blood vessels would not be detected as quickly in the absence of baroreceptors. since high blood pressure doesn’t cause symptoms, you probably won’t feel any different with a high blood pressure diagnosis.

who are humans' closest relatives, after primates? this question here addresses the controversy of whether humans are more closely related to bonobos or chimps. my question is - which group of animals are are closest relatives, beyond primates? or which group of animals are all primates most closely related to? who are humans' closest relatives, after primates? this question here addresses the controversy of whether humans are more closely related to bonobos or chimps. my question is - which group of animals are are closest relatives, beyond primates? or which group of animals are all primates most closely related to? after primates, the group of animals most closely related to humans—and to all primates—are known as "colugos" or "flying lemurs" (family cynocephalidae), which are part of the order dermoptera. although colugos are not true lemurs and do not actually possess the ability to fly, genetic and evolutionary studies have shown that they are the closest living relatives to primates. together, primates and colugos form a larger group called euarchonta, which also includes tree shrews (order scandentia). beyond the euarchonta, these mammals are most closely most sharks are cold-blooded. some, like the mako and the great white shark, are partially warm-blooded (they are endotherms). actually, the salmon shark is a warm-blooded shark. great white sharks are some of the only warm-blooded sharks. this allows them to swim in colder waters in addition to warm, tropical waters.

how do trees manage to grow equally in all directions? i was walking down a road with these beautifully huge trees when this question occurred to me. large trees with many thick branches have to grow equally in all directions, or they would tip over. is there some sort of mechanism to ensure this uniform growth? or is it just a happy coincidence arising from uniform availability of sunlight on all sides? if one branch of a tree becomes too heavy due to many sub-branches, does this somehow trigger growth on the opposite side of the tree? i have seen that potted plants in houses tend to grow towards sunlight. my mum often turns pots around by 180 degrees to ensure that plants don't bend in any one direction. i assume that this is because sunlight increases the rate of photosynthesis, leading to rapid growth of the meristem. for trees growing in open spaces, this wouldn't be a problem. but there are many large trees that grow in the shadow of buildings without bending away from the buildings, even though this is the only direction from which they would receive any sunlight. is there any explanation for this? how do trees manage to grow equally in all directions? i was walking down a road with these beautifully huge trees when this question occurred to me. large trees with many thick branches have to grow equally in all directions, or they would tip over. is there some sort of mechanism to ensure this uniform growth? or is it just a happy coincidence arising from uniform availability of sunlight on all sides? if one branch of a tree becomes too heavy due to many sub-branches, does this somehow trigger growth on the opposite side of the tree? i have seen that potted plants in houses tend to grow towards sunlight. my mum often turns pots around by 180 degrees to ensure that plants don't bend in any one direction. i assume that this is because sunlight increases the rate of photosynthesis, leading to rapid growth of the meristem. for trees growing in open spaces, this wouldn't be a problem. but there are many large trees that grow in the shadow of buildings without bending away from the buildings, even though this is the only direction from which they would receive any sunlight. is there any explanation for this? trees manage to grow in a relatively balanced manner due to a combination of biological mechanisms and their environment, though perfect symmetry is not always achieved and is not strictly necessary for their survival.

the primary driver of tree growth direction is **phototropism**, which is the tendency of plants to grow towards light. in open spaces where sunlight is available from all sides, trees tend to develop a roughly symmetrical canopy, as there is no single direction from which light predominantly comes. this allows the branches and leaves on all sides to receive similar amounts of sunlight, promoting even growth. however, in shaded areas or near buildings, trees often exhibit **asymmetrical gravitropism (also known as geotropism) is a coordinated process of differential growth by a plant in response to gravity pulling on it. roots show positive gravitropism and stems show negative gravitropism. that is, roots grow in the direction of gravitational pull (i.e., downward) and stems grow in the opposite direction (i.e., upwards).

what do you see when your eyes are closed? if you are in pitch black and you close your eyes, you sometimes can see strange shapes of various colors. a lot of the time these shapes and colors change as you observe them. this phenomenon still occurs if you are not in complete darkness. i am wondering what this is that you are `seeing'? is this phenomenon classified as seeing, as how can we be looking at something if our eyes are closed? if you are not seeing these shapes and colors, then what you observe is pure darkness. what is this pure darkness that you are seeing? since you are seeing it, is something stimulating the vision parts of your brain? is it the back of your eyelid? what do you see when your eyes are closed? if you are in pitch black and you close your eyes, you sometimes can see strange shapes of various colors. a lot of the time these shapes and colors change as you observe them. this phenomenon still occurs if you are not in complete darkness. i am wondering what this is that you are `seeing'? is this phenomenon classified as seeing, as how can we be looking at something if our eyes are closed? if you are not seeing these shapes and colors, then what you observe is pure darkness. what is this pure darkness that you are seeing? since you are seeing it, is something stimulating the vision parts of your brain? is it the back of your eyelid? when you close your eyes, whether in complete darkness or not, you often notice changing patterns, shapes, and colors behind your eyelids. these visual experiences are sometimes called "phosphenes." phosphenes occur due to spontaneous activity in your retina, which can generate signals even without light. other times, they can be caused by gentle pressure on your closed eyes or by random firing of cells in the visual system. essentially, your brain is interpreting electrical activity from the retina and visual pathways as if they came from light, even though there is none.

if you do not see any colors or shapes, but instead experience what seems eyes converts light into electrical signals. it contains rods (which help you see in low light) and cones (which help you see colors). macula. this small area of your retina is key to your vision. it’s responsible for the center of your visual field. it also helps you see color and fine details.

can a bacterium infect another bacterium? i researched about it by searching on google and reading some bacteriological articles, but i did not get any answer. i also asked some of my teachers, and they were also a bit confused. some said that it is possible and others said not. so the question remains: can a bacterium infect another bacterium? if so, how? can a bacterium infect another bacterium? i researched about it by searching on google and reading some bacteriological articles, but i did not get any answer. i also asked some of my teachers, and they were also a bit confused. some said that it is possible and others said not. so the question remains: can a bacterium infect another bacterium? if so, how? yes, a bacterium can "infect" another bacterium, but not in the same way that bacteria infect animals or humans. in microbiology, the infection of one bacterium by another is typically accomplished through a process called **bacterial conjugation** or through the action of certain genetic elements, like **bacteriophages** and **bacterial parasites**.

**bacterial conjugation** is a process by which one bacterium transfers genetic material to another through direct contact. during conjugation, a donor bacterium extends a structure known as a "pilus" to connect to a recipient bacterium and transfers plasmid dna janakiraman ramachandran argues that this complication can be avoided in those types of infection where this reaction is likely to occur by using genetically engineered bacteriophages that have had their gene responsible for producing endolysin removed.

why does an electrical shock freeze up muscles? why is it when someone comes into contact with an electrical supply that their body freezes up and is unable to move away from the electrical source? can someone explain this through a physiological perspective? why does an electrical shock freeze up muscles? why is it when someone comes into contact with an electrical supply that their body freezes up and is unable to move away from the electrical source? can someone explain this through a physiological perspective? when someone experiences an electrical shock, their muscles can "freeze up" and become unable to move away from the electrical source. this phenomenon occurs because electrical currents interfere directly with the normal electrical signals that control muscle movement in the body. from a physiological perspective, muscles contract in response to electrical impulses sent by nerves. when an external electrical current passes through the body, it can overwhelm and disrupt these natural nerve signals, causing all the muscles in the affected area to contract simultaneously and uncontrollably.

this involuntary, forceful contraction is known as "tetany." if the electricity is strong enough, the person loses voluntary control over their muscles, the loss of muscle control is the reason that a person may be unable to release themselves from the electrical source... electric injury occurs upon contact of a body part with electricity that causes a sufficient current to pass through the person's tissues. the injury depends on the density of the current, tissue resistance and duration of contact. a strong electric shock can often cause painful muscle spasms severe enough to dislocate joints or even to break bones.

what is 'protein' in food? i know that proteins perform a lot of functions and as a result there are a lot of different types of proteins in our bodies. when i eat food that has x grams of 'protein', what is this? a homogenous mix of proteins? a heterogenous one? is it specific set of them that are general enough to be used by my body despite coming from a different organism? how can it be sure that my body can use the kind of protein in the food? what is 'protein' in food? i know that proteins perform a lot of functions and as a result there are a lot of different types of proteins in our bodies. when i eat food that has x grams of 'protein', what is this? a homogenous mix of proteins? a heterogenous one? is it specific set of them that are general enough to be used by my body despite coming from a different organism? how can it be sure that my body can use the kind of protein in the food? when you see 'protein' listed on a food label, it refers to the total amount of protein content in that food, measured in grams. however, this protein is not a single, uniform substance. instead, it's a heterogeneous mixture of many different proteins naturally present in the food source (for example, the various proteins found in meat, beans, or dairy).

proteins are large, complex molecules made up of building blocks called amino acids. different foods contain different sets and proportions of proteins with varying arrangements of these amino acids, depending on their source (plant, animal, etc.). the specific proteins in food are generally unique to the organism protein is a nutrient needed by the human body for growth and maintenance. when broken down into amino acids, they are used as precursors to nucleic acid, co-enzymes, hormones, immune...

why does cracking a joint make noise? what causes the noise when you crack a joint? is joint cracking harmful? why does cracking a joint make noise? what causes the noise when you crack a joint? is joint cracking harmful? cracking a joint, such as your knuckles, produces a distinctive popping or cracking noise. this sound is caused by the rapid formation and collapse of tiny bubbles in the synovial fluid, which lubricates your joints. when you stretch or bend a joint, you increase the space within the joint capsule, reducing pressure on the synovial fluid. this pressure change allows dissolved gases (mainly carbon dioxide) in the fluid to form bubbles, which quickly collapse or "pop," creating the noise you hear.

joint cracking is generally not harmful if it is painless and does not cause any swelling or other symptoms. multiple studies have shown that the cracking mechanism and the resulting sound is caused by dissolved gas (nitrogen gas) cavitation bubbles suddenly collapsing inside the joints. this happens when the joint cavity is stretched beyond its normal size. the cracking of joints, especially knuckles, was long believed to lead to arthritis and other joint problems. however, this is not supported by medical research.

throughout evolutionary history, it is much more common for small animals to evolve into larger forms than the reverse. this trend is known as "cope's rule," which suggests a tendency for the body size of animals within a lineage to increase over evolutionary time. there are several reasons for this, including ecological advantages that larger size can confer, such as greater access to resources, improved thermoregulation, enhanced predator deterrence, and increased reproductive success.

major extinction events, such as the cretaceous–paleogene (k–t) extinction and the holocene extinction, have frequently resulted in the preferential loss of the largest animal species. cope recognised that clades of cenozoic mammals appeared to originate as small individuals, and that body mass increased through a clade's history. cope's rule, or the evolutionary trend toward larger body size, is common among mammals.

having two eyes, a condition known as binocular vision, is indeed a crucial factor in giving humans the ability to perceive depth in three dimensions (3d vision). this works primarily through a process called stereopsis, where each eye captures a slightly different image of the world due to their horizontal separation (about 6 cm apart on average in an adult). the brain then processes these two images and uses the differences between them (binocular disparity) to estimate how far away objects are.

however, having two eyes is not absolutely necessary for some forms of depth perception. people with vision in only one eye (monocular vision) can when your eyes work together correctly, they give you depth perception and 3d vision. they also give you color vision. having two eyes allows the brain to determine the depth and distance of an object, called stereovision, and gives the sense of three-dimensionality to the vision. both eyes must point accurately enough that the object of regard falls on corresponding points of the two retinas to stimulate stereovision; otherwise, double vision might occur.

the cell membrane is mostly made of something called a phospholipid bilayer: two layers of special fat molecules (phospholipids) aligned tail-to-tail. each phospholipid has a “head” at one end (which is hydrophilic, meaning it loves water) and two “tails” (which are hydrophobic, meaning they hate water).

when phospholipids are put in water (like in our cells), they naturally arrange themselves so the the cell membrane provides a barrier around the cell, separating its internal components from the extracellular environment. it is composed of a phospholipid bilayer, with hydrophobic internal lipid “tails” and hydrophilic external phosphate “heads.”

which part of human eyes is getting tired? it's a thing of common sense that if you read, drive or look at computer screen for too long your eyes will get tired. they burn, itch and try to close all the time. but i was wondering which part(s) of them need rest so much... blinking muscles? lense muscles? or the eyeball is drying out? which part of human eyes is getting tired? it's a thing of common sense that if you read, drive or look at computer screen for too long your eyes will get tired. they burn, itch and try to close all the time. but i was wondering which part(s) of them need rest so much... blinking muscles? lense muscles? or the eyeball is drying out? when your eyes feel tired after activities like reading, driving, or looking at a computer screen for an extended period, it is mainly due to fatigue in certain muscles of the eye, as well as some dryness. specifically, the ciliary muscles, which control the shape of the lens to help you focus on objects up close, work continuously during these tasks. over time, they become fatigued, making it harder to maintain clear focus. additionally, the extraocular muscles, which control the movement and alignment of your eyes, can also get tired, especially if you are focusing in the same direction for a long period.

another important factor is that eye experts believe that your focusing muscles may get stuck in 'near gear' from overusing them this way. it's no wonder that all this screen time can make eyes feel tired and dry.

how does yeast dissolve in sugar? there's this fascinating "kitchen experiment". take a "brick" of baker's yeast (not dry yeast), and a couple teaspoons of sugar, put them together in a cup and stir together. there's no doubt both are solids: yeast is a firm block, you must use quite a bit of force initially to crush it and even then it should remain a rather thick paste. the sugar is crystals, nothing tricky about that. and then, within a minute or so of stirring, your cup contains only liquid - not entirely fluid like water, but not much thicker than cream. solid+solid -> liquid. what process leads to that? what happens with the baking yeast when it comes in contact with sugar, that it turns from solid to liquid, and releasing enough water to dissolve the sugar? how does yeast dissolve in sugar? there's this fascinating "kitchen experiment". take a "brick" of baker's yeast (not dry yeast), and a couple teaspoons of sugar, put them together in a cup and stir together. there's no doubt both are solids: yeast is a firm block, you must use quite a bit of force initially to crush it and even then it should remain a rather thick paste. the sugar is crystals, nothing tricky about that. and then, within a minute or so of stirring, your cup contains only liquid - not entirely fluid like water, but not much thicker than cream. solid+solid -> liquid. what process leads to that? what happens with the baking yeast when it comes in contact with sugar, that it turns from solid to liquid, and releasing enough water to dissolve the sugar? when fresh (compressed) baker’s yeast is mixed with sugar in a cup and stirred, you witness a remarkable transformation: what begins as two solid substances quickly becomes a thick liquid. this happens due to a process called osmosis, which occurs at the cellular level.

baker’s yeast is made up of living yeast cells, which contain a high amount of water—about 70% of their mass. the solid “brick” texture comes from tightly packed cells that hold this water inside strong cell walls and membranes. sugar, on the other hand, consists of dry, crystalline molecules.

when you add sugar to the yeast and start mixing, yeast cells break down sugar through a process called fermentation. the main difference between compressed yeast and cream yeast is the solids content. compressed yeast solids vary between 28 and 35 percent yeast solids... cream yeast solids typically vary between 14 and 20 percent yeast solids...

the primary source of adenosine accumulation is the breakdown of adenosine triphosphate (atp), which is the main energy currency of the cell. when neurons are active, they consume a lot of atp to fuel their electrical and metabolic activity. as atp is used, it is sequentially broken down into adenosine diphosphate (adp) and then adenosine monoph once released, the availability and duration of atp is regulated by cell surface ectonucleotidases that hydrolyze atp, producing metabolites including adp and adenosine in the process. sleep pressure, or sleep homeostasis, is mostly mediated by the concentration of adenosine diphosphate (adp). adp is a waste product of neural activity, and high concentrations of adp are correlated with deeper slow-wave sleep.

why do adult insects have 6 legs? apparently, there is an advantage to having 6 legs in the insect world. what is that advantage, if anything? why would such an advantage exist for insects, but not for other, larger land animals? what do those middle legs do that the front and hind legs cannot, if anything? are they really essential, or do they just generate more muscle power? why do adult insects have 6 legs? apparently, there is an advantage to having 6 legs in the insect world. what is that advantage, if anything? why would such an advantage exist for insects, but not for other, larger land animals? what do those middle legs do that the front and hind legs cannot, if anything? are they really essential, or do they just generate more muscle power? insects have six legs primarily because of their evolutionary ancestry and the advantages that this body plan offers for survival. the six-legged, or hexapod, structure allows for efficient and stable movement over various surfaces. this is particularly useful for small, lightweight animals like insects. with three pairs of legs, an insect can use what’s called a "tripod gait," where it always has three legs on the ground at one time—two on one side and one on the other—providing both balance and continuous movement. this gait is very stable and makes it possible for insects to walk, run, and climb even on uneven or vertical surfaces insects must carefully coordinate their six legs during walking to produce gaits that allow for efficient navigation of their environment. different walking gaits have been observed to exist on a speed dependent continuum of phase relationships. in a metachronal wave gait, only one leg leaves contact with the ground at a time... the wave gait is often used at slow walking speeds and is the most stable, since five legs are always in contact with the ground at a time.
